evals.prompt: Our support team says customers get angry after failed invoice retries, but I cannot tell whether the real need is better messaging, retry scheduling, or admin tooling. Help me figure out what feature we should even define.
evals.expected_output: Routes to software-delivery/feature-discovery because the user problem and feature shape are still fuzzy and need sharpening before a formal artifact or implementation.

evals.prompt: Turn our account-recovery idea into a lean feature spec with goals, non-goals, acceptance criteria, and rollout guardrails.
evals.expected_output: Routes to feature-spec because the user explicitly wants a scoped feature artifact rather than discovery, review, or implementation.

evals.prompt: Design the delivery harness for this long-running app effort: decide when work stays in one session, when it should resume through compaction, and when to use a planner/generator/evaluator loop with hard handoff artifacts.
evals.expected_output: Routes to software-delivery/harness-design because the request is about cross-session delivery control and orchestration boundaries, not ordinary product planning or implementation.

evals.prompt: The agent keeps losing the original goal after roadmap execution and phase 1 implementation. Harden the workflow so compaction cannot make it drift away from the source objective.
evals.expected_output: Routes to software-delivery/harness-design because the request is about preserving goal lineage and cross-session execution control, not about building the feature itself.

evals.prompt: I already drafted a plan for scheduled exports. Challenge the scope, MVP cuts, rollout sequence, and whether the user value is strong enough before we commit engineers.
evals.expected_output: Routes to software-delivery/plan-product-review because the plan exists and the dominant need is product-value and scope challenge.

evals.prompt: Here is our multi-region write plan. Ignore the polished product pitch and red-team the architecture, rollback path, observability, tests, and failure modes before we start coding.
evals.expected_output: Routes to software-delivery/plan-engineering-review because the plan already exists and the dominant risk is engineering integrity under failure and rollout pressure.

evals.prompt: Review this onboarding redesign plan for flow clarity, empty and error states, interaction load, and accessibility before we build it.
evals.expected_output: Routes to software-delivery/plan-design-review because the user wants UX and interface critique of a plan before implementation.

evals.prompt: The team already built the customer-facing checkout redesign. Do not implement anything. Independently verify it in the browser, return a pass or fail verdict with evidence and defects, and tell us what must change before release.
evals.expected_output: Routes to software-delivery/frontend-evaluator because the browser-facing deliverable already exists and the main need is strict independent acceptance, not implementation or builder-side QA.

evals.prompt: Build the customer-facing pricing workflow in our Next.js app and verify desktop and mobile behavior in the browser yourself before handing it off for final signoff.
evals.expected_output: Routes to website-building because the main implementation risk is user-facing web behavior plus builder-side browser QA, not generic repo mechanics alone or independent evaluator signoff.

evals.prompt: Implement the retry queue in the existing service repo, add tests around the new backoff rules, and fix the failing migration script.
evals.expected_output: Routes to coding-and-data because the plan is stable enough for repo-backed implementation and test work.

evals.prompt: Before we ship the new permission model, give me a confidence check on blind spots, reversibility, and what we would want in a postmortem if this fails.
evals.expected_output: Routes to self-cognitive because the main need is risk-focused readiness and reflective challenge rather than discovery, planning, or coding.
